coredata.dc:title: Tuning nonradiative recombination loss by selective oxidation patterns of epoxy groups bound to different sites of graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Xue, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: A photocatalytic system with a high quantum yield is desirable for large-scale industrial applications such as the photovoltaic splitting of water. Owning to their tunable electronic and optical properties, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are promising for photocatalytic water splitting. Chemical functionalization modifies the electron distribution in the conjugated π system, which varies the optoelectronic properties of the GQDs. In the present work, density functional theory (DFT) and linear-response time-dependent DFT (LR-TDDFT) calculations are conducted to study the electronic structures and optical properties of GQDs with selective oxidation patterns of epoxy groups on their surfaces. Furthermore, the relationship between the oxidation patterns and the nonradiative decay rates are studied with Fermi’s golden rule approach. The results show that oxidation occurs more easily at the edge than in the core of the GQDs. The different binding configurations of the epoxy groups influence the structural and orbital symmetries of the graphene lattice, resulting in variations in the bandgaps and, consequently, the properties of the excited state. The nonradiative decay rates decrease with the distance from the core of the GQD. In particular, the ortho conformations exhibit faster nonradiative decay rates than the meta conformations, followed by the para conformations. Furthermore, the para conformations at the edge of the GQD demonstrate alternating nonradiative patterns, either accelerating or suppressing the nonradiative electron–hole recombination. The current computational work provides atomic insights for the rational design of photocatalytic systems based on GQD materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the different binding configurations of epoxy groups on the surface of a GQD. Ortho, meta, and para denote the bridging positions of the oxygen atom in the benzene ring. α, β, and γ denote identical bridging positions in different benzene rings. According to this notation, there are eight unique bridging positions of the epoxy group on the GQD surface, which are GQD-1: ortho-α; GQD-2: meta-α; GQD-3: para-α; GQD-4: ortho-β; GQD-5: meta-β; GQD-6: para-β; GQD-7: ortho-γ; and GQD-8: ortho-δ.

(a)–(h) The projected density of states (PDOS) for eight GQDs. The Fermi level is set to 0. The electronic states associated with atomic oxygen appear deep inside the valence band (VB) of GQD. The oxygen states in the conduction bands (CB) of the GQDs appear at much higher energy levels, which are not shown in the graphs due to the limited energy range.

(a) The highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) of oxidized GQDs. (b) The electron density distribution of HOMOs and LUMOs of the eight molecular systems.
(a)–(h) Normalized absorption spectra of oxidized GQDs. The square represents oscillator strengths.
(a)–(h) Variations of absorption spectra of oxidized GQDs with respect to various solvents.
Average C–C bond lengths (Å) and binding energies (eV) of the epoxy group attached to the GQD.
The calculated dipole moments of the lowest excited states of the oxidized GQDs in various solvents.
The calculated nonradiative decay rates and related parameters for the oxidized GQDs.

head.ce:title: Tuning nonradiative recombination loss by selective oxidation patterns of epoxy groups bound to different sites of graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, School of Textile Science and Engineering, Jiangnan University, No.1800 Lihu road, Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Textile Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, School of Textile Science and Engineering, Jiangnan University, No.1800 Lihu road, Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A photocatalytic system with a high quantum yield is desirable for large-scale industrial applications such as the photovoltaic splitting of water. Owning to their tunable electronic and optical properties, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are promising for photocatalytic water splitting. Chemical functionalization modifies the electron distribution in the conjugated π system, which varies the optoelectronic properties of the GQDs. In the present work, density functional theory (DFT) and linear-response time-dependent DFT (LR-TDDFT) calculations are conducted to study the electronic structures and optical properties of GQDs with selective oxidation patterns of epoxy groups on their surfaces. Furthermore, the relationship between the oxidation patterns and the nonradiative decay rates are studied with Fermi’s golden rule approach. The results show that oxidation occurs more easily at the edge than in the core of the GQDs. The different binding configurations of the epoxy groups influence the structural and orbital symmetries of the graphene lattice, resulting in variations in the bandgaps and, consequently, the properties of the excited state. The nonradiative decay rates decrease with the distance from the core of the GQD. In particular, the ortho conformations exhibit faster nonradiative decay rates than the meta conformations, followed by the para conformations. Furthermore, the para conformations at the edge of the GQD demonstrate alternating nonradiative patterns, either accelerating or suppressing the nonradiative electron–hole recombination. The current computational work provides atomic insights for the rational design of photocatalytic systems based on GQD materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as appealing zero-dimensional materials due to their tunable electronic and optical properties [1,2], biocompatibility [3], and photostability [4]. The carbon backbone defines the sp2-hybridized crystalline structure of the GQDs. Converting 2-D graphene into 0-D GQDs significantly modifies the electron distribution, resulting in size-dependent properties: the electronic and optical properties of a GQD are related to its size, surface state, and edge configuration. Changing the size and edge configuration leads to GQDs with different photophysical properties, such as photoluminescence (PL) [5], photocatalysis [6], electrical conductivity [7], and photoresponsivity [8]. Furthermore, the basal plane and edges of GQDs can be easily modified through chemical functionalization [9,10], which changes the sp2 domain [11] of the GQDs.

GQDs have great promise for catalyzing or co-catalyzing water-splitting reactions to generate eco-friendly power [12,13]. However, the quantum yields (QYs) of pristine GQDs are typically very low due to their large exciton binding energies and small dielectric constants [14], which reduce the probability of charge separation. To improve the QYs, reducing the nonradiative recombination in the GQDs is necessary. This reduction can be achieved through chemical functionalization. For example, the passivation of the GQD surface by polyethylene glycol increased the QYs from 13% to 28% [15]. The addition of amino groups has been shown to increase the QYs of GQDs to 29% [16]. Qu and co-workers have achieved an astonishing QY of 94% using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors [17]. The surface functionalization of GQDs can be precisely controlled by manipulating the reaction temperature [18-20], which changes the chemisorption position of a functional group on the surface of the GQD. For example, Niu et al. have found that a GQD surface shows different binding affinities toward atomic oxygens attached at different positions. Furthermore, an increased temperature can release the oxygen atom attached to the GQD surface [19], implying that a specific site on the surface of a GQD can be functionalized through controlled reaction temperatures.

The nonradiative recombination of electrons and holes is important for photocatalytic applications. Efficient nonradiative electron–hole recombination in low-lying excited states of GQDs is detrimental to the catalysis of chemical reactions due to the loss of electronic energy as thermal energy. However, a long-lived charge-separated state is beneficial for delivering more charges to the targeted reactant, improving catalytic efficiency. Reports on the nonradiative recombination loss in GQDs are very limited. Xu et al. have used single-particle spectroscopy to investigate the fluorescence of GQDs. They have found that high-lying defect states are much easier to transit into nonradiative trap states than low-lying ones. Therefore, the high-lying defect states have shorter emission lifetimes than the low-lying defect states [21]. Wang et al. have reported that the quantum confinement of functional groups on the basal plane of GQDs leads to a dark intrinsic state that appears below the first two bright states [22]. Cui has performed ab initio nonadiabatic molecular dynamic simulations to investigate the carrier relaxation dynamics of defect states in doped GQDs [11,23,24]. Nevertheless, there is still an insufficient number of studies exploring the relationship between chemical structures and nonradiative decay in GQDs.

Oxygen functional groups, such as epoxy, hydroxyl, and carboxy, are passivated on the basal plane or at the edges of GQDs [9,11,25,26]. The epoxy and hydroxyl groups are the main chemical species present at the basal planes of GQDs [27]. In particular, it is possible to oxidize the GQD surface with only an epoxy group [18,28-32]. These functional groups form sp3-hybridized bonds with the carbons, warp the planar structure of the carbon backbone, and break the integrity and the structural symmetry of the conjugated π system of the GQDs [33]. The specific surface sites where these oxygen-containing groups are attached can be used to tune the electronic structures and conductivity of graphene materials [31,32,34]. Therefore, it is possible to modulate the radiative or nonradiative electron–hole recombination by the selective oxidation of the GQD surface with chemisorbed oxygen atoms. The desired modulation can be achieved through thermal annealing or manipulating the tunneling current with scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) tips [18], enabling the fabrication of nanopatterned GQDs at the atomic scale. Precise control over the oxidative functionalization of GQD could be used to provide a favorable electronic structure that reduces the nonradiative recombination of electrons and holes. This structure will have improved photocatalytic efficiency because more charges are delivered to the targeted reactions. For example, a recent experimental study reported that functionalization of the GQD surface by electron-donating groups [35] provides better faradaic efficiency of CO2 electroreduction than functionalization by electron-withdrawing groups [18].

Although numerous experimental reports and theoretical calculations have demonstrated that the oxidation of GQDs changes the radiative recombination of electrons and holes, it is unclear how the interaction of oxygen-containing groups with the carbon backbone influences nonradiative recombination in GQDs. For example, it is uncertain whether the carboxyl group can act as a nonradiative recombination center in GQDs [36,37]. Furthermore, prior studies [38,39] have shown that varying the specific binding site on the basal plane of an oxygen-containing group significantly alters the optoelectronic properties of GQDs. In particular, an epoxy group bound to different surface sites of GQD has been shown to modify the intensities and positions of the low and main absorption peaks of GQDs [9]. Furthermore, passivating an epoxy group on a GQD surface induces the emission traps into GQDs, lowering or raising the QY in GQDs [25,27,40-43], depending on the specific binding site. Overall, unraveling the relationship between the oxidation configuration of the oxygen-containing groups and the corresponding nonradiative decay process of the low-lying excited states of GQDs provides atomic insight regarding the photocatalytic abilities of oxidized GQDs.

The two most popular approaches to modeling the nonradiative decay dynamics in nanomaterials are Fermi's golden rule [44,45] and ab initio nonadiabatic molecular dynamics (NAMD) [11,23,24,46]. The NAMD method relies on the explicit solution of the time-dependent Schrödinger equation coupled with nuclear dynamics, propagated using classical mechanics. However, applying this method to large molecular systems, such as GQDs, requires the electron and hole to be treated as independent particles to make it computationally affordable [46]. This approximation makes this method less accurate because of the exclusively large binding energies of excitons in GQDs. Even though NAMD performed within the linear-response time-dependent density functional theory (LR-TDDFT) framework takes into account the exciton effect [47,48], the computational cost may be prohibitively high due to the demanding procedure of solving the eigenvalue of a single-particle transition within the LR-TDDFT framework. Therefore, the practical application of NAMD based on LR-TDDFT is limited to very small molecules, such as benzene [49] and pyrazine [48].

In contrast, Fermi's golden rule approach, for example, Marcus theory [50] and its derivatives [51,52], is based on the analytically derived rate expressions that are computationally much cheaper than NAMD. The main parameters of the simulation, such as electronic coupling and vibrational frequencies, can easily be obtained by performing static calculations within the DFT framework. For example, Tong et al. have calculated the nonradiative decay rates in pincer gold(III) arylacetylide complexes [53]. They found it is essential to include the 3LLCT → S0 transition when calculating nonradiative decay rates. Li et al. have investigated the role of dangling bonds in the nonradiative recombination of electrons and holes in silicon quantum dots (QDs) [54]. Maggio et al. have studied the variation of nonradiative recombination of electrons and holes at the dye–TiO2 interface with respect to the conformational changes of the molecular geometry of the dye [55]. However, very few theoretical works have explored the nonradiative decay dynamics of GQDs [11,23,24,56] using NAMD. In particular, the effect of selective oxidation patterns on nonradiative decay dynamics in GQDs has not been previously reported. This lack motivates our model of the nonradiative decay dynamics of oxidized GQDs in order to fully understand its mechanism, especially the role of the specific binding site of an epoxy functional group in modulating the nonradiative decay rates of GQDs, which guides the design of GQD-based photovoltaic and photocatalytic devices.

In the present work, DFT and LR-TDDFT calculations are performed to examine the effect of the binding configurations of the epoxy group on the nonradiative decay dynamics of GQDs based on Fermi's golden rule. The electronic and optical properties of functionalized GQDs were studied by placing the epoxy group on eight unique binding sites on the surface of the GQD. The current results show that functionalizing the GQD surface with an epoxy group at different binding sites changes the electronic structure and absorption properties of the GQDs. In particular, different oxidation patterns break the structural and orbital symmetries, altering the nonradiative recombination processes of GQDs.

The nonradiative decay rates of GQDs can be obtained by applying Fermi's golden rule [57], which describes the transition probability between the excited state and the ground state per unit time. Under the Born–Oppenheimer and Condon approximations, the electronic and nuclear degrees of freedom can be treated separately. Therefore, by separating the nuclear and electronic wave functions, the nonradiative decay rate is given by:

The energies of the emission and the reorganization of solvent are obtained by the state-specific approach [54], which divides the electronic structure of the solvent into a fast component (electronic part) and a slow component (nuclear part). In the Franck-Condon region, there are two solvent environments, i.e., equilibrium and nonequilibrium solvents. For the equilibrium solvent environment, both the electronic and nuclear configurations of the solvent are balanced with either the excited state or the ground state of GQD. Meanwhile, for the nonequilibrium solvent environment, the electronic configuration of the solvent is balanced with the ground state of the GQD, and the nuclear configuration of the solvent is balanced with the excited state of the GQD. Thus, the emission energy is given by the energy difference between the excited and ground states. The energy of the excited state is calculated in the equilibrium solvent (

When the GQD transits from the lowest excited state to the ground state, the electronic configuration of solvent instantly adapts to the ground-state electronic configuration of GQD, while the nuclear configuration of solvent is unchanged. After both the electronic and nuclear configurations of solvent reach a balance with the ground-state electronic configuration of GQD, the reorganization energy of solvent is given by the energy difference of the ground states of GQD between the two stages. Even though there are other methods for obtaining the electronic coupling, such as fragment spin difference [61], fragment charge difference [62], and fragment molecular orbital [63], most electronic coupling calculations based on high-level ab initio theory are based on the GMH method, which is commonly used as the benchmark to evaluate the accuracy of other methods [64,65].

The initial structures of the GQDs are constructed by placing the epoxy functional group at eight unique positions on the GQD surface, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The GQD is hexagonal, as has been commonly detected in experiments [26]. Ortho, meta, and para denote the different bridging positions within a benzene ring, while α, β, γ, and δ denote identical bridging positions in different benzene rings. According to our notation system, there are eight unique conformations of the epoxy group on the surface of the GQD. For the zigzag-edged GQD, the ortho and para conformations appear both in-plane and at the edges of the GQD, while the meta conformations only appear in-plane. In particular, the first six conformations correspond to the basal-oxidized configurations, while the last three conformations correspond to the edge-oxidized configurations. For comparison, two pristine GQDs without adding any epoxy groups were also modeled, and their electronic and optical properties were calculated (Table S1). The pristine GQD-1 is the same size as the oxidized GQDs investigated in this study, while the pristine GQD-2 is similar to the GQDs synthesized experimentally [72].

The geometry optimization of the GQDs were carried out by the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE0) functional [59] at the 6–31 g* basis set and at the DFT level. Even though the global hybrid functionals are known to underestimate the charge-transfer excitations [58], the PBE0 functional can partially describe the charge transfer character of a conjugated
π
-bond system, similar to a range-separated hybrid functional [52]. Furthermore, a previous theoretical study has indicated that this combination of functional and basis set describes the excitation energies of graphene materials in good agreement with the experimental values [11,23,24,67,68]. After geometry optimization, frequency calculations were carried out to obtain the effective Huang–Rhys factor, effective intramolecular vibrational frequencies, and low-frequency reorganization energy based on equations (2)
-
(4). The absorption energies were obtained by performing normal LR-TDDFT calculations, and the state-specific approach was employed to obtain the emission energies of oxidized GQDs and reorganization energies of solvent. The absorption band was broadened with a Gaussian profile with a line width of 0.08 [69]. Finally, the nonradiative decay rate was computed using equation (4). The total density of states (DOS) and projected density of states (PDOS), i.e., the contribution of different chemical species to the DOS, can be estimated as follows [11,23]:

All calculations were performed with the conductor-like polarizable continuum model [66] using the Gaussian09 software package [70]. The dichloromethane is used as the solvent throughout the calculations.

The charge transfer length,
Δ R
, is used to characterize the extent of the exciton localization, calculated using the Multiwfn software [71]. A larger
Δ R
corresponds to a greater localization of excitons. A larger
Δ R
corresponds to a greater localization of excitons.

The average C–C bond lengths and the binding energies of the oxidized GQDs are listed in Table 1 . The average C–C bond lengths of the edge-oxidized configurations grew, while the average C–C bond lengths of the basal-oxidized configurations remain the same (GQD-1, GQD-4, GQD-5) or contract (GQD-2 and GQD-3) when compared to pristine GQD (Table S1). The binding energies of the edge-oxidized configurations are about two times greater than those of the basal-oxidized configurations, suggesting that oxidation occurs more easily at the edge than on the basal plane. This effect is because an oxygen functional group at the edge of a GQD causes a greater deformation of the conjugated π system, resulting in larger binding energies and longer C–C bond lengths than in the other configurations [39,73]. Furthermore, the variation of the binding energies with respect to the different binding configurations of the epoxy group on the GQD surface indicates different reactivities of the oxygen atom to the GQD surface. This is consistent with recent experimental observations [18,19] that the adsorption and desorption of oxygen atoms on the GQD surface can be manipulated by controlling the reaction temperature to achieve precise oxidative functionalization of the GQD surface. Additionally, increasing the GQD sizes is expected to have an insignificant effect on the qualitative trend of binding energies due to the minor effect of the size of the GQD on the lattice symmetry. Increasing the size of the GQD only increases the number of binding sites for epoxy groups, which has little effect on the GQD–epoxy interaction.

The PDOS (Fig. 2 ) show that the electronic states associated with the epoxy group appear deep inside the valance (VB) and conduction (CB) bands of the GQD; therefore, hybridization has a minor effect on the frontier molecular orbitals (MOs) of the GQD. However, the bandgaps of the GQDs vary with the oxidation patterns of the epoxy functional group (Fig. 3 (a)). There are two reasons for such variation. First, the epoxy group forms sp3-hybridization with the carbon backbone, causing the localization of electron densities around the binding site (Fig. 3(b)). Such localization breaks the double degeneracy of the highest occupied (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied (LUMO) molecular orbitals, resulting in variation of the bandgaps [33]. Second, the epoxy oxidation causes topographic protrusion around the binding sites (indicated by circles in Figure S1) due to the interruption of the conjugated π system, resulting in the redistribution of electron densities and variation of energy levels of frontier MOs. Such structural deformation is subtle in graphene [18]; however, it becomes significant in GQDs because the structure of a QD becomes more flexible with reduced size [74]. The perturbation of the oxygen functional group by the planar
π - π
interaction becomes more significant in GQD materials than in bulk materials. Such structural deformation is associated with the oxidation patterns of GQDs, as evidenced by the average C–C bond lengths for GQDs. Furthermore, the bandgaps of GQD-3 and GQD-6 are greater than the other configurations, suggesting that the oxygen atoms in the para conformation have a greater influence on the frontier MOs than the other conformations.

Fig. 4 shows the calculated absorption spectra of the oxidized GQDs. To verify the suitability of the PBE0 functional to the simulated system, the optical gaps of the S1 and S3 states of pristine GQD-2 were also calculated (Table S1). The calculated optical gap of S1 agrees very well with the experimental absorption edge of the pristine GQD [72]. Moreover, the calculated optical gap of the S3 state agrees with the experimental data; that is, 567 nm vs. 550 nm. However, the range-separated hybrid functional (LC-wPBE) severely overestimates the optical gaps of the S1 and S3 states, indicating that the PBE0 functional is a better choice for describing the excited states of the GQD materials. The optical gaps of the lowest excited states of the oxidized GQDs vary from 1.83 to 2.40 eV (Table S2), depending on the binding configurations of the epoxy groups. The main absorption peak is either blue- or redshifted depending on the specific binding site of the epoxy group on the GQD surface. The intensities of the main absorption peaks also vary with the binding position of the epoxy group on the surface of the GQD. This feature is due to the oxidative functionalization, which causes warping of the carbon backbone that breaks the structural and orbital symmetries in GQDs, resulting in electron redistribution to the frontier MOs and variation of the optical properties of GQDs. In particular, the absorption spectra of the para conformations (Fig. 4(c) and (f)) and the edge-ortho conformations (Fig. 4(g) and (h)) are blue-shifted compared to the pristine GQD (Figure S2).

Furthermore, a low-energy peak composed of two lower-bound bright states is split from the main absorption peak for all GQDs, due to the removal of the symmetrically degenerate S3 and S4 states [9] that are initially optically forbidden in pristine GQD [20]. Epoxy functionalization induces electron density localization around the binding site, that is, the defect state, which breaks the orbital symmetry and brightens the dark states. In particular, the para conformations have absorption intensities relatively lower than the other conformations, consistent with their larger bandgaps.

The Stokes shift characterizes the structural relaxation of the GQD and the dynamic reconfigurations of the solvent near the surface of the GQD. The results show that the Stokes shifts are sensitive to the binding positions of the epoxy group on the surface of the GQD (Table S2). The Stokes shift first decreases, then increases, with the distance from the core of the GQD. The smallest value of the Stokes shift is 0.03 eV (GQD-4), while the largest (GQD-7) is about four times greater than GQD-4, 0.13 eV. The relatively small values of the Stokes shifts suggest minor interruptions to the sp2 network of the graphene lattice during the relaxation of the excited state, indicating a relatively rigid structure. In contrast, the large values of the Stokes shifts cause the electron redistribution of the sp2 carbon-conjugated network during the excited state relaxation due to the relatively flexible structures of the GQDs.

The optical spectra, emission energy, and bandgaps of GQDs are not significantly affected by the number of epoxy groups functionalized on the surface of the GQD [73]. The optoelectronic properties of GQDs are more sensitive to the specific binding positions of the epoxy groups on the GQD surface than their coverage.

Fig. 5 shows the charge density differences between the excited and ground states for the lowest excited states of oxidized GQDs. A larger
Δ R
corresponds to a more localized electron density distribution, and vice versa [75]. In addition, the larger the
Δ R
, the lower the probability that the electron and hole recombine with each other, resulting in a lower absorption intensity. In particular, when
Δ R > 2
, the excited state is a charge transfer state; when
Δ R < 2
, the excited state is treated as an electron–hole pair with opposite charges [75]. The
Δ R
of the para conformations are greater than those of the meta conformations, followed by the ortho conformations. The
Δ R
of GQD-3 is>2, suggesting that the lowest excited state of GQD-3 is a charge transfer state. The bandgap of GQD-3 is the largest (Fig. 2(a)) among all of the GQDs studied. According to the energy gap law, a larger bandgap reduces the recombination of electrons and holes, resulting in a greater
Δ R
. The charge transfer lengths for the two para conformations (GQD-7 and GQD-8), both edge-oxidized, differ from one another. GQD-7 has the second-largest
Δ R
among all the GQDs studied, much greater than that of GQD-8 because of their different exciton characters. The CDD map shows no electron- or hole-densities in the epoxy group, indicating the intrinsic nature of electron–hole recombination in GQD-7. On the contrary, a sizable electron density is located in the epoxy group in GQD-8, suggesting that the electron–hole recombination in GQD-8 is mediated through the epoxy group. All the other configurations show exciton characters similar to GQD-8, suggesting that their electron–hole recombination is also related to the defect state induced by the epoxy group.

GQDs are usually synthesized in solvents, which affect the charge transfer by rearranging the dynamic configurations of the solvent molecules around the surface of the nanomaterials. A previous study [20] has shown that increasing the solvent polarity causes a redshift of the main absorption peaks of N-doped GQDs due to the increased screening effect, but it has an insignificant effect on the lower absorption peaks of N-doped GQDs due to the smaller change of the dipole moments of the low-lying excited states induced by the solvent polarity.

Our calculations show that oxidative functionalization also causes the redshift of the main absorption peaks of the GQDs, as discussed above. However, unlike the N-doped GQDs [20], the lower absorption peaks are sensitive to the oxidation patterns of the GQDs and the solvent environment. Adding solvent, e.g., dichloromethane, results in a redshift of the spectra and an increase in the intensity of the lower absorption peaks of the spectra to different degrees (Fig. 6 ). For example, the lower absorption intensity of the ortho-α configuration (GQD-1) is significantly increased in dichloromethane, while the intensity is only slightly increased in dichloromethane for the para-α configuration (GQD-3). This suggests that the influence of solvent polarity on the dipole moment of the excited state of GQD is sensitive to oxidation patterns. In particular, adding solvent increases the dipole moments of the lowest excited states of the oxidized GQDs (Table 2 ), except for the ortho-γ configuration. Strongly polar solvents, such as acetonitrile, slightly decrease the dipole moments of oxidized GQDs compared to weakly polar solvents, such as dichloromethane. The solvent reorganization energy is proportional to the square of the difference in the dipole moments between the ground and excited states [76], i.e.,
λ s
∝
(
μ
ex
-
μ
gs
)
2
, (where
λ s
is the solvent reorganization energy,
μ
ex
and
μ
gs
are the dipole moments of the excited state and ground state, respectively). Thus, an increase in the intensity of the lower absorption peak of the GQD indicates the increased solvent reorganization energy near the GQD surface or vice versa. As such, the solvent reorganization energy also depends on the oxidation patterns of the epoxy groups. Increasing solvent reorganization energy decreases the energy gap of the lowest excited state, reducing the number of quanta required for the high-frequency vibrational modes. It reduces the activation energy barrier [52] that appears in the last exponential term in the rate expression (4), accelerating the nonradiative charge transfer rate. Therefore, we expect the strongly polar solvents, such as acetonitrile, slightly suppress the nonradiative decay of oxidized GQDs compared to the weakly polar solvents, such as dichloromethane.

The low photocatalytic QYs of GQD materials are mainly caused by the nonradiative recombination between electrons and holes. The rapid loss of electronic energy to thermal energy greatly limits the applications of GQDs as photocatalytic agents.

Fig. 7 shows the calculated Huang-Rhys factors for the eight molecular systems. Only the high-frequency vibrational modes are shown since they are related to the defect state induced by the epoxy functionalization, while in the low-frequency range, the electronic transitions are coupled with the breathing modes of the graphene lattice [77]. In the high-frequency range, the nonradiative electronic transitions are coupled with the three main vibrational modes at around 1200, 1400, and 1700 cm−1. The vibrational modes around 1700 cm−1 correspond to C = O stretching [78]. The vibrational modes around 1400 cm−1 correspond to the D band of the GQDs [77] and result from the defect state due to the epoxy functionalization. The vibrational mode around 1200 cm−1 corresponds to C–H in-plane bending [79]. The vibrational intensity of the G band of GQDs, i.e., ∼1590 cm−1, is much weaker than the D band of GQDs, suggesting that nonradiative decay mainly occurs from the epoxy-induced defect states rather than the intrinsic state from the sp2-conjugated carbon backbone.

Table 3 lists the calculated nonradiative rate constants and related properties. The calculated rate constants are in the range of 0.1–1 ns−1, agreeing with the previous experimental measurements [22,72] and theoretical calculation results [77,80] of GQD materials. Except for the edge-para conformations (GQD-7 and GQD-8), the order of the magnitudes of the nonradiative rate constants is
k
ortho
>
k
meta
>
k
para
. This trend is consistent with their charge transfer lengths, that is,
Δ
R
para
> Δ
R
meta
> Δ
R
ortho
, since a greater charge transfer length reduces the recombination probabilities of electrons and holes in GQDs [68,75]. Furthermore, the nonradiative rate constants of the edge conformations are smaller than the in-plane conformations of the GQDs, suggesting that the nonradiative recombination rate decreases from the center to the edge of the GQD. This is similar to N-doped GQDs, in that the center N-doping induces trap states that lead to radiationless decay, while the edge N-doping has little effect on the bandgap of the GQDs [20]. In particular, the rate constant of GQD-7 is 10–100 times smaller than the other structures and drastically different from GQD-8, suggesting that the oxidation at certain edge-para positions in GQD results in a prolonged nonradiative decay.

The rate expression (4) shows that the nonradiative decay rate is related to the emission energy, reorganization energy, vibrational frequency, and electronic coupling. The electronic coupling appears in the numerator of the rate expression and is therefore proportional to the nonradiative decay rate. A smaller emission energy and a larger reorganization energy (solvent and intramolecular reorganizations) increase the last exponential term in the rate expression (4), reducing the activation energy barrier for nonradiative decay and thus accelerating the radiationless transition [52]. In addition, the higher vibrational frequency accelerates the nonradiative electronic transition. According to the time-dependent Schrödinger equation, the nonradiative recombination of electrons and holes are proportional to the nonadiabatic coupling vectors [23,24]. The nonadiabatic coupling vector of the lowest excited state can be written as [11]:
(12)

where

are the excited- and ground-state wave functions, respectively;

is the nuclear velocity; and

is the multi-dimensional nuclear coordinate. Equation (12) suggests that the nonadiabatic coupling vector depends on the nuclear velocity. The faster the nucleus moves, the faster the electrons and holes recombine. Therefore, higher vibrational modes accelerate the radiationless transition from the excited state to the ground state.

The order of the magnitudes of the electronic couplings is
V

V

>
V
para
(except for GQD-7 and GQD-8), consistent with the nonradiative decay rates for the GQDs. This ordering suggests that electronic coupling plays a dominant role in the nonradiative decay of oxidized GQDs. However, at the edge, the electronic coupling of GQD-8 becomes much larger than for the other GQDs, significantly enhancing its nonradiative decay rate. The solvent reorganization energy decreases from the core to the edge of the GQD. In addition, the intramolecular reorganization energies of the edge-para conformations are much smaller than the other conformations. Thus, GQD-7 has the smallest reorganization energy, significantly suppressing its nonradiative decay rate. As such, when the epoxy group is on the edge-para positions, it exhibits alternating nonradiative decay rates that either accelerate or suppress the radiationless transition from the lowest excited state to the ground state.

Overall, the nonradiative electron–hole recombination is significantly affected by the oxidation patterns of GQDs, which are unavoidable during the synthesis of GQD materials. The variation of the oxidation patterns introduces different amounts of disorder into the conjugated π system, resulting in the localization of electrons [11,81]. The localized electronic density breaks the molecular orbital symmetries, changing the optical properties and affecting the nonradiative electron–hole recombination.

In the present work, we perform DFT and LR-TDDFT calculations to investigate the effect of oxidation patterns of epoxy groups on the electronic structures and optical properties of GQDs. We further analyze the nonradiative decay processes of GQDs with epoxy groups attached at different surface sites, based on Fermi's golden rule. The epoxy group shows various binding stabilities for the binding sites of GQD. In particular, oxidation occurs more easily at the edges than in the basal plane of GQDs due to the greater deformation of the carbon backbone caused by the edge functionalization. The different binding configurations of the epoxy group on the GQD surface interrupt the conjugated π system of the carbon backbone, inducing defect states. These defect states break structural and orbital symmetries, causing variation of bandgaps and optical spectra. In particular, the oxidation effect has a greater influence on the frontier MOs in para conformations than the other conformations.

The absorption spectra of para- and edge-ortho conformations are blue-shifted compared to the pristine GQD, while they are redshifted for the other conformations. The oxidation of pristine GQD removes the degeneracy of the main absorption peak, resulting in the emergence of low absorption peaks in oxidized GQDs. Due to the oxidation effect, these lower-bound peaks originate from the brightening of optically dark states in pristine GQD. The para conformation, in particular, has a lower absorption intensity than the other conformations. The separation of electrons and holes in the lowest excited state is also sensitive to the binding configurations of the epoxy group. In particular, the para conformation shows the greatest extent of separation, followed by the meta conformation, then the ortho conformation. The solvent environment leads to redshifts of the absorption spectra and increased absorption intensities, which depend on the oxidation patterns of the epoxy groups. However, a strongly polarized solvent slightly decreases the dipole moments of the lowest excited states of oxidized GQDs compared to a weakly polarized solvent, which suppresses the nonradiative decay.

Due to the sensitivity of frontier MOs and the lowest excited states of oxidized GQDs to the binding configurations of epoxy groups, the nonradiative recombination of electrons and holes in the lowest excited state is also dependent on the oxidation patterns of GQDs. In particular, the nonradiative electron–hole recombination for the ortho conformation is faster than for the meta conformation, followed by the para conformation. The para conformation exhibits alternating nonradiative decay rates at the edges, either accelerating or suppressing nonradiative electron–hole recombination. A faster nonradiative electron–hole recombination reduces the available charge densities for catalyzing the chemical reactions. In comparison, an inefficient nonradiative electron–hole recombination provides more carrier densities to reach the targeted reaction, improving the photocatalytic efficiency. Therefore, the photocatalytic efficiency of GQD materials can be tuned by selectively oxidizing their basal planes, which show heterogenous nonradiative decay rates depending on the specific binding site on their surfaces.
